text: In the operating room, the surgeon performs a laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy. Endoscopy reveals a normal gastric sleeve and significant narrowing of the duodenum at the point where the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) crosses it. The transverse colon is reflected to identify the middle colic vessels, duodenum, and SMA. The peritoneal lining of the duodenum is incised, and the duodenum is mobilized distally and proximally. A jejunal limb, approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, is prepared for anastomosis, with a stay suture placed to orient the anastomosis.

The surgeon creates enterotomies in the duodenum and jejunum, forming an anastomosis using a linear stapler while carefully avoiding damage to the superior mesenteric artery at the distal tip. A stay suture is placed posteriorly to orient the common enterotomy, and the defect in the anastomosis is over-sown in two layers with running Vicryl suture.
The endoscope is reinserted to inspect the anastomosis intraluminally, and a leak test is performed using methylene blue. The anastomosis appears intact, with no visible leaks.
